text: This article examines manifest interparty conflict over policy issues and the strategic role of coalition agreements in resolving these disputes. We present empirical findings on the structure of coalition agreements—specifically regarding policy compromises—and the resulting interparty conflicts that emerge throughout the lifespan of governments in Germany, Belgium, Italy, and the Netherlands. Our analysis explores the extent to which governing parties are constrained by formal, written agreements on disputed topics. The evidence shows that coalition agreements across all four nations contain specific policy mandates, one-third of which are framed with high precision. While these deals cover both consensual and controversial issues, our central finding reveals that when internal conflicts arise, parties almost invariably revert to the original agreement if a relevant provision exists. Consequently, the policy statements embedded in coalition agreements serve as a vital mechanism for facilitating stable decision-making across diverse political systems.
